pattern: For every index used as the 'peak', the cheapest mountain through it pairs the smallest strictly-smaller value on its left with the smallest strictly-smaller value on its right; tracking running minimums in one left-to-right and one right-to-left pass keeps this linear.
hints: A mountain triplet is just three indices i < j < k where nums[j] is strictly greater than both its chosen neighbors; fix the middle index and ask what the best left and right partners are.
For a candidate peak at j, the cheapest valid left partner is the minimum value anywhere before j that is still less than nums[j], and symmetrically for the cheapest right partner after j.

Precompute running minimums left-to-right and right-to-left in O(n); a position qualifies as a peak only if both of those minimums are strictly less than its own value, and its triplet sum is their total. No nested loop is needed.

edge_inputs.description: mountain requires skipping over non-adjacent indices
strictly decreasing then increasing, no interior peak qualifies
minimum length of three, forms a valid mountain
minimum length of three, not a mountain
all equal values, no strict inequality holds anywhere
many candidate peaks, cheapest combination is not the first one found

stress.description: small random arrays to cross-check against the O(n^3) oracle
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(3, min(size, 60))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 100000000) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]

stress.description: large random array exercising the linear scan at scale
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(3, min(size, 100000))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 100000000) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]
stress.note: not cross-checked against the cubic oracle at this size; the linear solution is the source of truth here
